title: How to Add Whimsy in Your Life

chapters.title: Introduction
chapters.text: You want more than just working and repeating the same routine, right? That extra something is called whimsy. It means small, playful moments that make your day feel alive. Maybe it’s taking a new way home, hiding a treat for yourself, or simply looking up for a minute. Kids are naturally full of whimsy, but adults often lose it and call it being responsible. However, you can’t just add whimsy to a week that leaves you exhausted. Two things get in the way: Feeling too tired to want it and being too busy to make space for it. This guide helps you tackle both. First, deal with the stress that’s worn you out. Then, find the time that’s already yours. After that, you can start adding whimsy back into your day, even today. Try each step as you go. Start with one today, and by the end, you’ll let delight become a regular part of your routine.

chapters.title: Clear the stress to unlock whimsy
chapters.text:
Whimsy needs extra energy and a relaxed mind, but stress leaves you with neither. When you’re tired, play is the first thing to disappear. Delight can even feel like just another demand when you’re already stretched thin. Rest by itself doesn’t solve the problem because your body needs movement to finish the stress cycle. Sitting at a desk keeps that stress stuck, turning it into tiredness. That’s why collapsing on the couch or scrolling your phone doesn’t really help, because you still feel drained. These things seem restful but don’t clear what your body is holding. So, try moving on purpose right after work, before you settle in for the evening. If you wait until you’re on the couch, the stress stays. Letting out a little stress each evening works better than saving it all for the weekend. Basically, you have two main choices: Burn off stress with movement, or focus on a simple, repetitive skill to distract your mind. What you choose depends on what works for you. Free options are just as good. Try a walk without your phone, pacing around your home, doing push-ups at the counter, or dancing to three songs in your kitchen. You could knead bread, do slow pilates, knit, or wash dishes by hand. If you have access, swimming, gardening, or playing an instrument can help, but none of these are required. If even that feels like too much, just sit and breathe in for five counts and out for ten. Do this three times, then tense all your muscles and let go. You’ll notice it working. You start to feel less tired, and your shoulders relax. That feeling is the extra space whimsy needs. Many of these activities also give you hands-on, offline time that makes your week feel richer. Just twenty minutes can clear stress and start to fill the space it leaves behind.

A tired mind shuts out play. Give your body a rest, or even play can become draining.

Dive deeper with “Burnout: The Secret to Unlocking the Stress Cycle” by Emily Nagoski and Amelia Nagoski.

chapters.title: Claim the free hours for whimsy
chapters.text:
Once you’ve cleared your stress, you’ll have the energy for whimsy. You might think you don’t have the time, but you probably have more than you realize. It feels like your time disappears because it’s hidden in small gaps throughout your day. When you treat every task as urgent, those gaps close up before you can use them. Brigid Schulte, the author of Overwhelmed: Work, Love, and Play When No One Has the Time, recalls a time when she thought she had no free time, but when she tracked her week, she found 28 hours of unnoticed leisure. If you take a good, scrutinized look at your week, you’ll find you also have these chunks of ten minutes that you can use for a good ol’ whimsy. Find one of those time chunks now. Write down how you’d spend a free hour this week without limiting your imagination. Maybe visit a cafe with a notebook, browse a bookstore without a list, stroll through a museum, cook a meal you’re proud of, or try a pottery or dance class. You don’t need a big city or lots of money. A library can replace a bookstore, a free gallery can stand in for a museum, and a borrowed how-to book can take the place of a paid class. No matter your location or budget, a good hour should do at least two of these: Help you relax, sharpen your taste, or get you off your screen. Pick something that fits, put it on your calendar, and protect that time like you would any important meeting. Don’t wait for the day when you feel caught up, because, for bad or for good, it will not come. Your inbox will always have something in it, and the perfectly clear desk is a myth. The hour you set aside for whimsy matters, even if your work isn’t finished. Protect small moments, too. Do one task at a time, then pause. Let yourself daydream in the gaps between tasks.

Treating whimsy as essential as a work meeting is a token of self-respect.

Dive deeper with “Overwhelmed: Work, Love, and Play When No One Has the Time” by Brigid Schulte.

chapters.title: Design whimsy into your day
chapters.text:
Now comes the fun part. You have energy and an hour to use it. Whimsy is about enjoying things just for the sake of it, like those silly pleasures adults often skip. It’s what makes a dull week feel alive again. Repetition makes life feel flat because your brain tunes out what it expects, and the fix is to add a small, unexpected twist to your day. Begin with a surprise for your future self. Hide a treat or a couple dollars in your coat pocket and forget about it. Later, you’ll find it and feel a spark of joy. Put a single flower in a glass on your desk. Drink your coffee from a different mug just for fun. Let yourself play for no reason. Count the red doors on your way to the store. Step only on the dark paving stones. Dance during every chorus while you cook dinner. The fact that it’s pointless is what makes it work. This is play, just like children do. Next, look for wonder. Visit the tower or big wheel you usually walk past and look out for five minutes. If there’s no tower, try a rooftop, hill, or top-floor window. The night sky is always free, so step outside tonight and watch it for three minutes without using an app to name the stars. If a child shows you a strange stick they say is magic, join in their excitement and skip the explanation. Choose one idea and try it tonight. Give yourself a few minutes of whimsy and notice how it brings color back to your day.

Routine erases the days. A single act of whimsy can help you look at your day from a new perspective.

Dive deeper with “Joyful: The Surprising Power of Ordinary Things to Create Extraordinary Happiness” by Ingrid Fetell Lee.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text: Whimsy is a skill, and like any skill, it needs space to grow. You create that space by dealing with stress and taking back your own time. The delight you thought you lost is just three minutes and a little courage away. It only asks for a few minutes, not money or a special place. Clear your stress today, book an hour for yourself tomorrow, and add one small, fun thing to that time. Your week will feel different as soon as you start playing with it.

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
Follow these steps in order, starting today.

• Today: Take a 20-minute walk without your phone, go for a swim, or dance to your personally curated playlist. Let your body signal that the workday is over.
• Tomorrow: Write down a hobby or outing you’d spend an hour on. Put it on your calendar and protect that time like you would any important meeting.
• When you have that hour, make a whimsy box: Write down as many small, silly delights as you can, one action per slip of paper. For example, wear your favorite socks on a dull day, take the long way home, or buy yourself a flower. Put the slips in a jar, and on a dull day, pick one and do it right away.

For more ways to recharge, free up your time, and bring play back to ordinary days, keep exploring the Headway app.

Read it till the end?
